Title: Quantitative exploration of the catalytic landscape separating divergent plant sesquiterpene synthases

Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Structure-based combinatorial protein engineering (SCOPE) mutagenesis of 9 residues; recombinant expression in E. coli; in vitro enzyme assay; GC-MS product profiling; kinetic analysis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: TEAS catalyzes formation of 5-epi-aristolochene as the dominant sesquiterpene product from FPP; mutational variants alter product specificity toward 4-epi-eremophilene, premnaspirodiene, and minor sesquiterpenes
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful as a model enzyme for engineering sesquiterpene product specificity and exploring evolvability of defense-related terpenoid metabolism; specific residue combinations can redirect product outcome
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclizes farnesyl diphosphate through carbocation-mediated rearrangements to predominantly form 5-epi-aristolochene
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TEAS is one parental sesquiterpene synthase used to define the catalytic landscape. 2. A library of all 2^9 residue combinations in the TEAS background showed broad accessibility of alternative sesquiterpene product profiles. 3. Many mutants retained near-wild-type catalytic rates while shifting product specificity, indicating strong epistasis and mutational plasticity.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: TEAS and HPS share ~75% amino acid identity; eight of nine interconverting substitutions are achievable by single nonsynonymous nucleotide changes
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologously expressed in E. coli for biochemical assay
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: 5-epi-aristolochene is a precursor of antifungal phytoalexins in solanaceous plants
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Most mutation combinations changed product specificity without strongly reducing kcat, but some variants were inactive or not recovered

Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Prior structure-guided interconversion with TEAS; comparative sequence analysis; product profiling context in this study via HPS-like residue combinations in TEAS background
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: HPS specifies premnaspirodiene formation from FPP; HPS-like activity is reachable through multiple residue combinations in the TEAS background
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Provides a template for engineering alternative sesquiterpene outputs and identifying multiple mutational solutions for HPS-like chemistry
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclizes farnesyl diphosphate through carbocation-mediated rearrangements to predominantly form premnaspirodiene
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Sesquiterpene cyclization branch leading to premnaspirodiene
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HPS is the divergent parental ortholog contrasted with TEAS. 2. Multiple combinations of nine functionally important residues in the TEAS background generated HPS-like product spectra. 3. Full native HPS shows higher premnaspirodiene specificity than the TEAS M9 variant, implying additional residues beyond the nine contribute to specificity.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Native henbane enzyme reportedly produces ~97% premnaspirodiene, whereas TEAS M9 produced ~72% and one M8 mutant produced ~81%
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologously expressed in E. coli for biochemical assay
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Premnaspirodiene is a precursor of antifungal phytoalexins in solanaceous plants
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Specificity in engineered backgrounds does not fully match native HPS, indicating background dependence and epistasis

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Engineered sesquiterpene synthase; terpene cyclase intermediate between TEAS and HPS
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Targeted amino-acid substitution of TEAS active-site residues; recombinant expression; GC-MS product profiling
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Two active-site substitutions in TEAS redirect deprotonation/neutralization to generate 4-epi-eremophilene, an intermediate hybrid activity that is rare across the M9 library
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Illustrates that limited active-site engineering can create non-natural sesquiterpene outputs and mechanistic intermediates for metabolic engineering
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclizes farnesyl diphosphate and redirects final deprotonation-neutralization to produce 4-epi-eremophilene
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Alternative sesquiterpene cyclization branch leading to 4-epi-eremophilene
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. EES is an engineered intermediate activity derived from TEAS. 2. EES-like enzymes are rare in the M9 library. 3. EES activity shows a strict dependence on the T402S active-site mutation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: 4-epi-eremophilene was reported as a terpene not previously identified in nature in the earlier interconversion study; in this library EES-like enzymes formed a sparsely populated subgroup
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologously expressed in E. coli for biochemical assay
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: No specific biological function stated; product represents an alternative sesquiterpene hydrocarbon outcome
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Rare activity in sequence space; may not reflect natural metabolic deployment
